video_title: "We Don't Trust Agents" - What This CTO Knows That You Don't
gist: Merge's co-founders (CEO Shensi and CTO Gil) detail how an integrations/infrastructure company repositioned around enterprise AI: agent connectivity (Merge Agent Handler / MCP), model routing and cost control (Merge Gateway), and hard guardrails against agentic data exfiltration. They argue agents can't be trusted once connected to tools, that AI-generated code is creating a supply-chain security crisis, and that the real 2026 inflection is enterprises finally moving from 'ChatGPT in the browser only' to governed, audited agent deployment.

insights.claim: Merge made a deliberate ~18-month pivot built on three bets — using AI aggressively internally, re-aiming its existing unified-API product at AI companies, and launching net-new AI products — and the founders credit those bets for accelerating the business.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)
insights.so_what: A template for how an incumbent SaaS-infrastructure vendor stays relevant in the agent era; the pivot working is the bull case for Merge's valuation.

insights.claim: Merge now sells three products: Merge Unified (data sync/normalization to power enterprise search and RAG), Merge Agent Handler (an MCP server giving hundreds of integrations to internal and customer-facing agents), and Merge Gateway (model-routing infrastructure with a security layer plus cost-saving and smart-routing policies).
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)
insights.so_what: Maps the three monetization layers — data plumbing, agent connectivity, and model orchestration — where integration vendors are now competing.

insights.claim: Merge Agent Handler functions as a single central node through which all of an employee's AI tools (Claude Code, Codex, ChatGPT, Perplexity) connect to underlying SaaS, letting a CTO see all activity, catch security violations, and revoke or grant access centrally.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CTO (Gil)
insights.so_what: Positions Merge as the governance/control plane for enterprise agent access — a potentially defensible chokepoint as agents proliferate across tools.

insights.claim: Merge builds its own connector/tools even when no public MCP server exists for a target platform, removing integration coverage as a blocker for customers.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)
insights.so_what: Breadth of connectors is the moat in integration-aggregation; willingness to hand-build closes the long-tail gap that MCP alone leaves open.

insights.claim: Merge offers granular per-field/per-action permissions on connected systems (e.g., letting a PR intern read Salesforce accounts but be hard-blocked from writing or deleting any data), filling a gap where native tools force an all-or-nothing access choice.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)
insights.so_what: Fine-grained access control is a concrete differentiator and a buying trigger for regulated enterprises adopting agents.

insights.claim: Most enterprises in 2025 restricted employees to ChatGPT in the browser with no tool connections, but in 2026 buyers are actively demanding governed agent deployment, marking a real adoption inflection that Merge says it is positioned to monetize now.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CTO (Gil)
insights.so_what: If the 'agents actually get deployed' inflection is real, 2026 is the year agent-infrastructure revenue ramps — a timing signal for the whole category.
insights.quote: This year we're now starting to see companies being like, "We need this. We need this."

insights.claim: Enterprise trust in cloud-hosted AI is cyclical: GenAI's arrival triggered no-AI-training and no-cloud contract clauses, those reservations eased as buyers used and trusted models, but strong residual demand persists for AI running in customers' own infrastructure rather than multi-tenant cloud.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CTO (Gil)
insights.so_what: Vendors offering self-hosted/single-tenant deployment options win regulated enterprise deals; pure multi-tenant SaaS faces a procurement headwind.

insights.claim: AI-era buyers purchase integration infrastructure without knowing what they need — replacing the old SaaS buyer with seven API experts grilling on rate limits with buyers who ask for a diagram and just want to know if it works with their software.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)
insights.so_what: Sales now requires consultative hand-holding and prescriptive best-practices, shifting GTM toward forward-deployed/solutions motions and raising the value of vendor expertise.

insights.claim: Large AI companies buy far faster than financial-services or SaaS buyers because competition is so fierce, and they often arrive having already built Merge's agent into a working proof-of-concept before the first sales call.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)
insights.so_what: AI-native buyers compress sales cycles dramatically; vendors serving them see faster revenue recognition but must support proactive, self-serve evaluation.

insights.claim: The most common enterprise agent connections are general productivity tools — ticketing, file storage (Google Drive, Box, Dropbox), messaging, email, and code repositories — with function-specific pull for accounting (QuickBooks, NetSuite, Xero) and marketing (HubSpot).
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)
insights.so_what: Shows where agent automation is landing first; these connected systems-of-record are both the demand drivers and the data-exfiltration risk surface.

insights.claim: A CTO of a large regulated financial firm told Merge his employees pressure him to allow agent automation ('all my friends are using agents') but compliance blocks connecting internal services to external ones — a demand-vs-governance gap Merge sells into.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CTO (Gil)
insights.so_what: Pent-up internal demand colliding with regulatory constraint is the precise wedge for agent-governance products; identifies the buyer and the pain.

insights.claim: Enterprises increasingly require agent-access governance to tightly couple with identity providers so that when an employee leaves, their agent access is immediately revoked.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CTO (Gil)
insights.so_what: Identity-provider integration is becoming table stakes for agent-governance vendors; flags IAM/SSO players as adjacent beneficiaries.

insights.claim: Merge's core security thesis is that agents are inherently untrustworthy and require hard guardrails and blocks — not just rules — because a non-deterministic agent told to move data can leak sensitive fields like social security numbers that a human-reviewed deterministic pipeline never would.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CTO (Gil)
insights.so_what: Frames the buy case for data-loss-prevention/guardrail layers around agents; 'we don't trust agents' is the product positioning for the whole category.
insights.quote: We don't trust agents. We say, "Hey, we can try to set rules, but there need to be hard guard rails and blocks for things like sensitive data being sent across."

insights.claim: Agentic coding is flooding GitHub with pull requests faster than humans can review, so malicious code slips into widely-used open-source packages, and Gil argues this supply-chain vector caused recent attacks and will worsen as AI-generated code grows.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CTO (Gil)
insights.so_what: Supply-chain security tooling and software-composition-analysis vendors face structurally rising demand; a second-order risk to every company depending on OSS.
insights.quote: Agents are pushing a ton of code. You don't have enough humans to read all that code and so things are slipping by.

insights.claim: An agent is harmless until connected to tools — 'an evil genius locked in a jail cell' — so the act of giving agents tool access, which every company is now racing to do, is precisely where security fails.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CTO (Gil)
insights.so_what: A clean mental model for where agent risk concentrates: the tool/integration boundary, which is exactly Merge's product surface.
insights.quote: The second you connect it to tools, which is what everyone is trying to do right now, that's where everything goes wrong.

insights.claim: AI is making breaches both easier to create and easier to find — Gil cites Whiz reportedly finding (via AI, not manual review) a vulnerability where a single git push of a file could grant access to every repository hosted on GitHub.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CTO (Gil)
insights.so_what: Good models will surface long-dormant vulnerabilities in 20-30-year-old packages; both attackers and defenders scale, raising baseline breach frequency.

insights.claim: While on-call, Gil personally intervened against over a thousand bot signups in an hour actively scanning Merge's backend endpoints from IPs worldwide, illustrating that automated attacks are now constant and a single success can mean the end of a company.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CTO (Gil)
insights.so_what: Bot-detection and endpoint-protection are now operational necessities, not nice-to-haves; quantifies the rising automated-attack volume.
insights.quote: All it takes is one and it could mean the end of your company.

insights.claim: Gil argues a company's biggest security threat is internal — phished accounts, the wrong hire, or a planted spy — so Merge prioritizes background checks and reference calls over external port-scanning defenses.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CTO (Gil)
insights.so_what: Reframes security spend toward insider-threat and identity controls; relevant for HR-vetting and identity-governance vendors.

insights.claim: Attackers now overwhelmingly hold companies' reputations hostage rather than monetizing stolen data directly — they blackmail the breached firm to prevent a leak, because notification obligations and brand damage make the company value the data far more than the buyer does.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CTO (Gil)
insights.so_what: Explains ransomware/extortion economics and why breached firms quietly pay; cyber-insurance and incident-response demand follows this dynamic.

insights.claim: AI removes the labor and language barriers for global cybercrime — attackers who previously lacked English or coding skills now 'speak English perfectly, write code perfectly, and have unlimited manpower,' which Gil expects to drive more attacks.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CTO (Gil)
insights.so_what: Structural increase in the attacker base and capability; durable tailwind for the entire cybersecurity sector.
insights.quote: They now speak English perfectly, they now write code perfectly, and they now have unlimited manpower, all driven by AI.

insights.claim: Enterprise sales for large SaaS vendors is structurally weakening because the time and cost to rebuild the same product in-house has collapsed, eroding renewal-negotiation leverage as customers credibly threaten 'I could just build this.'
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)
insights.so_what: Bearish read on incumbent SaaS pricing power and renewals; a core driver of the 'SaaS apocalypse' debate and public-market SaaS volatility.

insights.claim: Rising consumer/employee expectations for built-in automation are squeezing legacy SaaS, because buyers no longer want to purchase a platform and build agents on top — they expect agentic automation out of the box.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)
insights.so_what: SaaS vendors that don't ship native agent automation risk losing to AI-native or buildable alternatives; a feature-gap thesis for shorting laggards.
insights.quote: The SAS platform cannot keep up with the expected automation. It's just hard to compete.

insights.claim: Merge's counter to SaaS disruption is a forward-deployed model: off-the-shelf software customized cheaply by AI to exact needs still beats rebuilding from scratch because it spares buyers the nuance and detail that fall outside raw coding time.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CTO (Gil)
insights.so_what: The bull case for surviving SaaS — value migrates to vendors who pair ready-made software with low-cost AI customization, i.e., a forward-deployed engineering motion.

insights.claim: Both founders predict 'English becomes your programming language': as models ask clarifying questions and build robust systems, a plain-English instruction becomes a reusable, deterministic, infinitely-repeatable artifact, making agent-harness and workflow-builder platforms largely unnecessary.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CTO (Gil)
insights.so_what: Bearish on standalone agent-orchestration/workflow-builder startups; the durable value is auditability and observability, not reliability tooling.
insights.quote: All that's going to matter is just can you type it in English somewhere, have that run on a periodic cadence for your background agents. Um, and then do you have auditability and observability for security?

insights.claim: The founders are bullish on Salesforce's headless (UI-less, API/MCP-driven) push, arguing its 20-year open-API ecosystem proves the moat was always the data layer, not the UI, so agents doing everything via API only deepens that lock-in.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CTO (Gil)
insights.so_what: Contrarian to the 'agents kill SaaS UIs' narrative for Salesforce specifically — frames it as a winner if data remains the system of record.

insights.claim: Companies must adopt headless/API-first because agents (and tools like Claude Code picking packages) will increasingly choose vendors automatically, so making signup, account creation, and payment frictionless for agents is now competitive necessity.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)
insights.so_what: Agent-driven vendor selection is a new distribution channel; products not consumable headlessly by agents risk being designed out of the buying decision.

insights.claim: Trust-based categories — infrastructure and security products — remain an exception to agent-driven, no-human purchasing, because buyers still want to meet a vendor and gauge reliability before committing.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)
insights.so_what: Defines which software categories retain a human, relationship-led sales motion versus those headed toward agent-automated procurement.

insights.claim: Marc Benioff is unlikely to be counted out because Salesforce has stayed dominant for 30 years through multiple tech shifts, which the founders attribute partly to his 'beginner's mind' philosophy of constantly reimagining the company from scratch.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)
insights.so_what: A defense of Salesforce's incumbency through the AI transition; relevant to anyone weighing legacy-SaaS survivorship.

insights.claim: The 'token-max' productivity push is real but the AI bill hitting the CFO is far worse than expected — companies may double productivity without doubling budget — driving heavy demand for cost-control via routing products like Merge Gateway.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CTO (Gil)
insights.so_what: AI-spend governance is an emerging budget line; FinOps-for-AI and model-routing/gateway vendors have a clear tailwind.

insights.claim: Margin discipline is rising across AI products — Cursor notably expanded its margins, which is very hard to do — and cheap open-source models are often the optimal route for trivial queries, though automating that routing is difficult.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)
insights.so_what: Validates intelligent model routing (cheap models for easy tasks) as a margin lever; Cursor cited as proof AI-app margins can improve.

insights.claim: Gil's hot take is that many companies over-engineer ML by building and training custom models while their product lags, when using a generic model and improving the product would serve customers better.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CTO (Gil)
insights.so_what: Buy-vs-build signal: custom-model investment often destroys value; favors foundation-model consumers over in-house training efforts.

insights.claim: Merge's founders warn that many AI companies are raising at 3,000x — or routinely hundreds-to-thousands-x — revenue multiples, and based on revenue figures they know firsthand, most will fail or be unable to raise their next round and forced into massive layoffs.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)
insights.so_what: An insider's bearish call on a coming AI-startup washout; flags refinancing risk and layoffs as the failure mechanism, not just valuation compression.
insights.quote: We're looking at thousandx to hundred-to-thousandx multipliers. A lot of these companies are going to fail.

insights.claim: Public and private AI markets tell very different valuation stories, and the founders expect a 'merging' of the two when OpenAI and Anthropic eventually go public.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)
insights.so_what: OpenAI/Anthropic IPOs would be the forcing event reconciling private and public AI valuations — a watch item for the whole sector's re-rating.

insights.claim: Merge serves over 20,000 (possibly ~25,000) self-served organizations and over 400 enterprise customers, including OpenAI, Perplexity, Netflix, Uber, Mistral, Dropbox, Freshworks, Brex, JP Morgan, US Bank, Ramp, Drata, and Vanta.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)
insights.so_what: A concrete enterprise/AI-lab customer roster signaling Merge's strategic position in the AI integration stack and its blue-chip logo coverage.

insights.claim: Merge grew revenue significantly last year while barely increasing headcount, which the founders attribute to AI-driven leverage on the business.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)
insights.so_what: Evidence that internal AI adoption can decouple revenue growth from headcount — the efficiency thesis investors look for in AI-native operators.

insights.claim: Merge powers core, mission-critical functions (login flows, AI model data lookups) for major customers at four-to-six-nines uptime, where an outage could lose a customer all of its own customers overnight — and the founders note some companies have recently collapsed this way.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CTO (Gil)
insights.so_what: Deep operational embedding raises switching costs (a moat) but also concentrates systemic risk; reliability is the product for infrastructure vendors.

insights.claim: Merge runs the business on capital-efficiency metrics — gross margins, cash burn, cash-burn multiple, and runway reviewed weekly — alongside revenue, and treats being the #1 product in its market as non-negotiable.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)
insights.so_what: Signals a disciplined, burn-multiple-focused operating posture consistent with a company positioning for durability through an AI-startup shakeout.

insights.claim: Merge climbed a 'logo ladder' — using each successive customer's reputation to win the next size tier — and made a concerted post-Series-B push to move up-market with a more mature sales motion and enterprise-ready product.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CTO (Gil)
insights.so_what: A repeatable enterprise GTM playbook for infrastructure startups; reference-driven up-market motion as the path to blue-chip logos.

insights.claim: Merge makes AI usage a cultural mandate tied to performance reviews across all functions — R&D, accounting, recruiting, finance, marketing all work in Claude Code — and screens in interviews for candidates who want to use AI, not necessarily those already cutting-edge.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)
insights.so_what: A concrete model for enforcing org-wide AI adoption; the 'AI usage in performance reviews' practice is a leading indicator of AI-native operating leverage.

insights.claim: Merge applies Keith Rabois's 'barrels vs ammunition' framework but argues AI shifts hiring toward more barrels, since one person with Codex or Claude Code can now do what previously required a manager plus a team of specialists.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CTO (Gil)
insights.so_what: AI changes the optimal org shape toward high-agency generalists; a hiring thesis for the AI-native staffing model.

insights.claim: Merge lets engineers expense whatever AI coding tools they prefer (Codex, Claude Code, or others) subject only to security reviews, treating tool choice as individual preference rather than standardizing.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)
insights.so_what: Signals genuine, broad usage of both Codex and Claude Code inside an AI-forward company — a demand data point for both tools.

insights.claim: The founders prize 'missionaries over mercenaries' and weed out candidates chasing hot-company status, top-percentile cash with low burn, or instant senior titles — warning that hot-company hires abandon ship when things get hard.
insights.speaker: guest, Merge CEO (Shensi)
insights.so_what: A retention-risk lens for evaluating hot AI startups: teams stacked with status-chasing mercenaries are fragile in downturns.
